ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for instant noodles as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for prepared noodle dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard add-on portion of boiled instant noodles weighing approximately 100 g, typically served alongside Yong Tau Foo or Laksa as an extra carbohydrate component.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Noodles, Chinese, cellophane or long rice (mung beans), cooked / Noodles, egg, cooked, enriched; adapted to instant noodle profile using Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient reference for instant noodles
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input values (energy 450 kcal, protein 9 g, fat 18 g, carbohydrate 60 g, sodium 1200 mg, saturated fat 9 g per 100 g) are consistent with published profiles for fried-then-boiled instant wheat noodles with seasoning packet. USDA FoodData Central entry for 'Noodles, instant, dry' (FDC ID 172013) reports energy ~436 kcal, protein ~9.5 g, fat ~17 g, carbohydrate ~63 g per 100 g dry weight, closely corroborating the input values. Singapore HPB Foodo database lists instant noodles (cooked) at approximately 150–160 kcal per 100 g cooked; however, the input values reflect the dry/pre-cooked block weight basis consistent with USDA dry-weight data. Values retained from input with USDA corroboration. Sodium 1200 mg/100 g is consistent with seasoning-inclusive instant noodle products documented in USDA Branded Food entries.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Instant Noodles — University of Sydney GI Database; Instant noodles (various brands, boiled) GI ~47–52
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database reports GI values for instant noodles (boiled) in the range of 47–52 (mean ~52, low GI category). The value of 52 is used as a conservative upper-bound estimate for this add-on portion. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 60) / 100 = 31.2 for a 100 g serving. Despite the low-to-medium GI, the high carbohydrate content per 100 g results in a high glycemic load, which is clinically significant.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1200 mg per 100 g — double the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold; saturated fat 9 g/100 g further impairs vascular compliance
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this food; if consumed, discard the seasoning packet entirely and do not consume the broth, which can reduce sodium intake by approximately 40–50%.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load of 31.2 per 100 g serving driven by 60 g refined carbohydrate; despite a low-to-medium GI of 52, the large carbohydrate density causes significant postprandial glucose excursion
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion to no more than 50 g dry weight and pair with non-starchy vegetables and a lean protein source to blunt postprandial glucose response; lower-GI noodle alternatives such as soba or shirataki are preferred.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 9 g/100 g (50% of total fat) from palm oil raises LDL cholesterol; sodium 1200 mg/100 g elevates blood pressure; trans fat 0.1 g/100 g present; ultra-processed formulation associated with increased CVD risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of instant noodles; the combination of high saturated fat, very high sodium, and trans fat makes this food incompatible with a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Palm oil-derived saturated fat 9 g/100 g is pro-inflammatory; sodium 1200 mg/100 g exacerbates tissue oedema; no anti-inflammatory micronutrients present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid instant noodles due to the strongly pro-inflammatory saturated fat and sodium profile; if consumed occasionally, add anti-inflammatory ingredients such as ginger, turmeric, or leafy greens.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Instant Noodles (Add-On Portion), known in Chinese as 加方便面 (jiā fāng biàn miàn), is a supplementary serving of pre-fried, dried wheat noodles that are boiled to order and added to Singaporean and Malaysian hawker dishes such as Yong Tau Foo and Laksa. The noodles are made from refined wheat flour, palm oil, salt, and seasoning, classifying them as an ultra-processed food. When boiled, they have a soft, slightly chewy texture and absorb the flavour of the accompanying broth or sauce. Per 100 g (dry-weight basis), this food delivers 450 kcal, 9 g protein, 18 g fat (of which 9 g is saturated fat from palm oil), and 60 g carbohydrate with 3 g dietary fibre. Sodium content is exceptionally high at 1200 mg per 100 g, primarily from the seasoning packet. The glycemic index is approximately 52 (low-to-medium), but the glycemic load per 100 g serving is 31.2, which is clinically high. This food is unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease due to its very high sodium, high saturated fat, and high glycemic load. It is vegetarian and halal-suitable but contains gluten. It is commonly ordered as a paid add-on at hawker stalls across Singapore and Malaysia.
common_user_queries: Can I add instant noodles to my Yong Tau Foo?
How many calories in add instant noodle?

confidence_reason: Input nutrient values are corroborated by USDA FoodData Central data for dry instant noodles (FDC ID 172013) and are consistent with published profiles for palm-oil-fried instant wheat noodles with seasoning. The exact brand and formulation are not specified, introducing moderate uncertainty. Sodium value of 1200 mg/100 g is consistent with seasoning-inclusive instant noodle products in USDA Branded Food entries. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database range for boiled instant noodles.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this specific instant noodle product is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database at the product level; only total fat and saturated fat are reliably reported. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific instant noodle add-on product are not available in approved sources at the required specificity. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — instant noodles made from wheat flour and palm oil contain no dietary cholesterol; confirmed per USDA FoodData Central entry for instant noodles (FDC ID 172013). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — no vitamin C is present in wheat-flour-and-palm-oil instant noodles; confirmed per USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are based on dry-weight (pre-cooked block) basis consistent with USDA FoodData Central dry instant noodle data. Cooked-weight values would be substantially lower per 100 g due to water absorption. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was not used as a primary nutrient source as it does not constitute an approved primary source under Project Veritas v2 policy; USDA FoodData Central was used as the primary corroborating approved source. The food is described as vegetarian in the input; this is plausible for plain instant noodles without meat-based seasoning, but many instant noodle seasoning packets contain animal-derived flavourings — this flag should be verified against the specific product.
